agent1_snippets.content: Oral cavity hygiene should be maintained.  This may not always be possible because the endotracheal tube may not allow for meticulous cleaning at the back of the throat. agent1_snippets.content: Chlorhexidine gluconate mouthwash and topical corticosteroids have been proven to decrease the severity of aphthous ulcers and shorten their duration. agent1_snippets.title: Gingivitis -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: If it is a drug-induced gingival overgrowth, the physician can change the medication to improve the outcome of treatment of the condition. If it is due to a nutritional deficiency, supplements can be prescribed. Medications in the form of antiseptic mouthwash that contains chlorhexidine can also be prescribed in conjunction with the mechanical removal of plaque. It has been suggested that the use of chlorhexidine mouthwashes in addition to the usual toothbrushing and interproximal cleaning leads to a significant decrease in the build-up of dental biofilm. The concentration of the chlorhexidine rinse does not affect its effectiveness. [19]

agent1_snippets.title: The Role of Fluoride on Caries Prevention -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Fluoride mouthwash helps prevent dental caries by increasing fluoride levels in dental plaque and saliva. [19] The mouthwash must be used at a different time of the day than when toothbrushing to increase its availability in the biofilm and optimize its topical effect. [28] The concentration depends on the frequency of use. Over-the-counter solutions of 230 ppm sodium fluoride are indicated for daily rinsing, and those containing 900 ppm for weekly use. [29] They are not recommended for children under 6 years due to the risk of swallowing, as the risk/benefit ratio favors risk. [6] Parents must supervise the use of fluoride mouth rinses in permanent dentition, as this has proven to be more efficacious than unsupervised utilization. Fluoride Varnish, Gels, and Foams

agent1_snippets.content: Other antiseptic agents such as aloe vera and honey have been suggested. Aloe vera is inexpensive, soothing, and easy to apply. agent1_snippets.title: Periodontal Disease -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Antibiotics may be administered locally or systemically in cases that do not respond to mechanical therapy. Chlorhexidine gluconate is a commonly used antimicrobial agent that complements mechanical periodontal treatment. This agent is typically administered as a mouth rinse, but can also be applied as a gel, varnish, or subgingival chip. When used alongside regular toothbrushing, chlorhexidine helps reduce plaque buildup, making it highly effective in treating chronic periodontitis. A relatively new advancement in pharmacotherapy for periodontal disease involves the use of a chlorhexidine gluconate chip, which is inserted into the periodontal pocket after cleaning. This chip provides a long-term, sustained release of chlorhexidine gluconate into the affected area, enhancing treatment effectiveness. [36] [37] [38]

agent1_snippets.title: Paronychia -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Paronychias are usually either treated with incision and drainage or antibiotics. If there is inflammation with no definite abscess, treatment can include warm soaks with water or antiseptic solutions (chlorhexidine, povidone-iodine) and antibiotics. Warm soaks should be for 10 to 15 minutes, multiple times a day. There is not strong evidence recommending topical vs. oral antibiotics, and this may be physician-dependent based on experience. Antibiotic used should have staph aureus coverage. Topical antibiotics used may be a triple antibiotic ointment, bacitracin, or mupirocin.  In patients failing topical treatment or more severe cases, oral antibiotics are an option; dicloxacillin (250mg four times a day) or cephalexin (500mg three to four times a day). Indications for antibiotics with anaerobic coverage include patients where there is a concern for oral inoculation; this would require the addition of clindamycin or amoxicillin-clavulanate. If the patient has risk factors for MRSA (including but not limited to: recent hospitalization, recent surgery, ESRD on hemodialysis, HIV/AIDS, IVDU, resident of long term care facility), chose an antibiotic with the appropriate coverage. Options include trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole DS (1 to 2 tablets twice a day), clindamycin (300 to 450mg four times a day) or doxycycline (100mg twice a day). [3]

agent1_snippets.content: Treatment of the acute phase aims to halt tissue destruction and to control the patient's discomfort. This involves gentle, ultrasonic debridement of superficial gingival plaques and calculi along with localized oxygen therapy, directed at necrotic lesions. [5] The use of 0.12% chlorhexidine gluconate mouth rinse should be considered, a suggested regimen being twice a day for 30 days. [9] Systemic antimicrobials are considered in the acute phase in cases with poor response to debridement or those with symptoms of systemic involvement, including fever, malaise, vomiting, etc. [5]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1377
agent2_snippets.content: Chlorhexidin is linked to a wide range of medicated shampoos that incorporate chlorhexidine gluconate as a primary active moiety, often combined with additional antifungal or keratolytic agents. Specific formulations in which chlorhexidin has the active moiety include “chlorhexidine gluconate 10 MG/ML / ketoconazole 2.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / ketoconazole 10 MG/ML / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / ketoconazole 10 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [Vital‑Ketodine],” “chlorhexidine gluconate 20 MG/ML / miconazole nitrate 20 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [MiconaHex+Triz],” “chlorhexidine gluconate 30 MG/ML / climbazole 5 MG/ML / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine gluconate 40 MG/ML / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo,” and “chlorhexidine gluconate 40 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo [Vital‑Chlor].” In addition, chlorhexidin is identified as an ingredient in several broader shampoo products, namely “chlorhexidine / climbazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / ketoconazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / ketoconazole Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / miconazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” “chlorhexidine / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo,” and the simple “chlorhexidine Medicated Shampoo.” Across all these products, chlorhexidine (often in the form of chlorhexidine gluconate) serves as a key antiseptic component, while the accompanying agents such as ketoconazole, miconazole nitrate, climbazole, and salicyloyl phytosphingosine provide antifungal or keratolytic effects, creating combination therapies aimed at treating scalp infections, dandruff, and related dermatologic conditions.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 702
agent2_snippets.content: Medicated Shampoo is a therapeutic topical preparation offered in numerous dose‑form variants that are defined by specific active‑ingredient combinations and concentrations. The primary antiseptic moiety, chlorhexidine (often as chlorhexidine gluconate), appears in a wide range of formulations, including chlorhexidine 10 mg/mL, 20 mg/mL, 23 mg/mL, 30 mg/mL, and 40 mg/mL. Chlorhexidine is combined with antifungal agents such as ketoconazole (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL, marketed as Ketochlor), miconazole nitrate (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL, marketed as Malaseb or MiconaHex+Triz), and climbazole (e.g., chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL). Anti‑inflammatory hydrocortisone is incorporated in multi‑agent products (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL). Keratolytic and barrier‑supporting agents such as salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 mg/mL) are added to several chlorhexidine‑based shampoos (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL). Stand‑alone chlorhexidine preparations (e.g., chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL, Vital‑Clor) are also listed. Additional medicated shampoos contain other active agents: miconazole alone or with salicylic acid (e.g., miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL / salicylic acid 20 mg/mL), chloroxylenol 10 mg/mL combined with miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL and salicylic acid 20 mg/mL, urea 100 mg/mL, and zinc pyrithione 3 mg/mL (the latter includes the inactive ingredient alpha‑tocopherol). These diverse formulations deliver antiseptic, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, keratolytic, and moisturizing effects, targeting scalp infections, fungal dermatoses, inflammatory conditions, hyperkeratosis, and general dermatologic disorders.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1058
agent2_snippets.content: Chlorhexidine (chlorhexidin) gluconate is the core antiseptic ingredient in a wide range of medicated shampoos, formulated at concentrations from 10 mg/mL to 40 mg/mL. These shampoos are combined with antifungal agents—ketoconazole (10 mg/mL) in products such as chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (branded Keto‑C, Vital‑Ketodine) and chlorhexidine 23 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (Ketochlor); miconazole nitrate (20 mg/mL) in chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL (Malaseb, MiconaHex+Triz); and climbazole (5 mg/mL) in chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL. Anti‑inflammatory hydrocortisone (10 mg/mL) is incorporated in triple‑combination formulas such as chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL. The keratolytic and skin‑conditioning agent salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 mg/mL) appears alone with chlorhexidine or in multi‑agent blends (e.g., chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL; chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL). Additional antiseptic bases such as chloroxylenol (10 mg/mL) are used in formulations like chloroxylenol / miconazole / salicylic acid and chloroxylenol 10 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL / salicylic acid 20 mg/mL. Other dose forms within the medicated shampoo category include miconazole‑only shampoos, urea 100 mg/mL shampoo, and chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL shampoo (Vital‑Chlor). Collectively, these varied dose forms illustrate how medicated shampoos can be tailored to provide antimicrobial, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, and keratolytic therapy for dermatologic and otic conditions, with specific brand names and concentration combinations defining each product’s therapeutic focus.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
agent2_snippets.content: Chlorhexidin (chlorhexidine gluconate) is the core antiseptic active moiety in a broad range of medicated shampoos that are formulated to treat scalp infections, fungal overgrowth, bacterial colonization, and scaling dermatologic conditions. The product class “Medicated Shampoo” includes numerous dose forms that combine chlorhexidine at concentrations of 10 MG/ML, 20 MG/ML, 30 MG/ML, or 40 MG/ML with antifungal agents such as ketoconazole (2.5 MG/ML or 10 MG/ML), miconazole nitrate (20 MG/ML), climbazole (5 MG/ML), or with keratolytic and soothing agents such as salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 MG/ML) and salicylic acid (18 MG/ML). Specific branded formulations include Keto‑C and Vital‑Ketodine (chlorhexidine 20 MG/ML + ketoconazole 10 MG/ML), Malaseb and MiconaHex+Triz (chlorhexidine 20 MG/ML + miconazole nitrate 20 MG/ML), and Vital‑Chlor (chlorhexidine 40 MG/ML). Additional combinations listed are chlorhexidine 20 MG/ML + ketoconazole 10 MG/ML + salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML, chlorhexidine 30 MG/ML + climbazole 5 MG/ML + salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML, chlorhexidine 40 MG/ML + salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 MG/ML, and chlorhexidine alone at 10 MG/ML, 20 MG/ML, or 40 MG/ML. The generic category also encompasses shampoos that pair chloroxylenol 10 MG/ML with miconazole nitrate 20 MG/ML and salicylic acid 20 MG/ML, as well as stand‑alone medicated shampoos containing miconazole, urea 100 MG/ML, or zinc pyrithione 19 MG/ML. The dose form “chlorhexidine / ketoconazole / salicyloyl phytosphingosine Medicated Shampoo” links the product class to this specific triple‑active combination. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the extensive use of chlorhexidine as a central antiseptic component across diverse medicated shampoo formulations designed to provide targeted antimicrobial, antifungal, keratolytic, and soothing effects for scalp and dermatologic therapy.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
agent2_snippets.content: Ketoconazole is a widely used antifungal agent incorporated in numerous medicated shampoos (e.g., Nizoral 10 mg/mL and 20 mg/mL, Keto‑C, Vital‑Ketodine, Ketochlor, Pharmaseb, and generic ketoconazole shampoo). In many formulations it is combined with the broad‑spectrum antiseptic chlorhexidine gluconate (10–40 mg/mL), the topical corticosteroid hydrocortisone (5 mg/mL, 10 mg/mL, or hydrocortisone acetate 5 mg/mL), the keratolytic/skin‑conditioning agent salicyloyl phytosphingosine (0.5 mg/mL), chloroxylenol, or other antifungals such as miconazole nitrate (20 mg/mL) and climbazole (5 mg/mL). Representative combination products include chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (Keto‑C, Vital‑Ketodine), chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / ketoconazole 10 mg/mL (Ketochlor), chlorhexidine 20 mg/mL / miconazole nitrate 20 mg/mL (Malaseb, MiconaHex+Triz), chlorhexidine 30 mg/mL / climbazole 5 mg/mL / salicyloyl phytosphingosine 0.5 mg/mL, chlorhexidine 40 mg/mL (with or without salicyloyl phytosphingosine), chloroxylenol / ketoconazole, chloroxylenol / miconazole nitrate / salicylic acid, and hydrocortisone‑only shampoos (5 mg/mL or acetate 5 mg/mL). Zinc pyrithione 19 mg/mL is also marketed as an antibacterial/anti‑seborrheic medicated shampoo. The “medicated shampoo” dosage form is a topical scalp preparation intended for fungal, bacterial, and seborrheic scalp disorders, delivering antiseptic, antifungal, anti‑inflammatory, and keratolytic actions. The extensive range of active ingredients and concentrations allows clinicians to tailor therapy to the specific microbial, fungal, inflammatory, or keratolytic needs of individual patients. From a safety standpoint, topical ketoconazole‑containing shampoos are contraindicated in patients receiving the systemic agents dihydroergotamine mesylate, dihydroergotamine tartrate, or ubrogepant due to potential adverse drug interactions.
